Â«
Distress Tolerance Handout 9 (Distress Tolerance Worksheets 7â€“7b)
Improving the Moment
A way to remember these skills is the word IMPROVE.
With Imagery:

Â‰ Imagine very relaxing scenes.

Â‰ Imagine a secret room within yourself. Furnish
it the way you like. Close and lock the door on
anything that can hurt you.

Â‰ Imagine everything going well.

Â‰ Make up a calming fantasy world.

Â‰ Imagine hurtful emotions draining out of you
like water out of a pipe.

Â‰ Remember a happy time and imagine yourself
in it again; play out the time in your mind
again.

Â‰ Other:
With Meaning:

Â‰ Find purpose or meaning in a painful situation.

Â‰ Focus on whatever positive aspects of a
painful situation you can find.

Â‰ Repeat these positive aspects in your mind.

Â‰ Remember, listen to, or read about spiritual
values.

Â‰ Other:
With Prayer:

Â‰ Open your heart to a supreme being, God, or
your own Wise Mind.

Â‰ Ask for strength to bear the pain.

Â‰ Turn things over to God or a higher being.

Â‰ Other:
With Relaxing actions:

Â‰ Take a hot bath or sit in a hot tub.

Â‰ Drink hot milk.

Â‰ Massage your neck and scalp.

Â‰ Practice yoga or other stretching.

Â‰ Breathe deeply.

Â‰ Change your facial expression.

Â‰ Other:
With One thing in the moment:

Â‰ Focus your entire attention on just what you
are doing.

Â‰ Keep yourself in the moment.

Â‰ Put your mind in the present.

Â‰ Focus your entire attention on the physical

Â‰ Listen to a sensory awareness recording (or
use Distress Tolerance Handout 9a)

Â‰ Other:
With a brief Vacation:

Â‰ Give yourself a brief vacation.

Â‰ Get in bed; pull the covers up over your head.

Â‰ Go to the beach or the woods for the day.

Â‰ Get a magazine and read it with chocolates.

Â‰ Turn off your phone for a day.

Â‰ Take a blanket to the park and sit on it for a
whole afternoon.

Â‰ Take a 1-hour breather from hard work.

Â‰ Take a brief vacation from responsibility.

Â‰ Other:
With self-Encouragement and rethinking the situation:

Â‰ Cheerlead yourself: â€śYou go, girl!â€ť â€śYou da
man!â€ť

Â‰ â€śI will make it out of this.â€ť

Â‰ â€śIâ€™m doing the best I can.â€ť

Â‰ Repeat over and over: â€śI can stand it.â€ť

Â‰ â€śThis too shall pass.â€ť

Â‰ â€śI will be OK.â€ť

Â‰ â€śIt wonâ€™t last forever.â€ť

Â‰ Other:
List (and then practice) rethoughts that are particularly important in your crisis situations (e.g., â€śThe fact that he did
not pick me up doesnâ€™t mean he doesnâ€™t love meâ€ť):

Â‰

Â‰

copyright_notice: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Improving the Moment A way to remember these skills is the word IMPROVE. With Imagery: 

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
